subject: Physics
text: Magnetic dipole: An arrangement of two unlike poles of equal strength and separated by a small distance is called a magnetic dipole. In SI, the unit of magnetic pole strength is ampere metre (A.m).
text_html: Magnetic dipole: An arrangement of two unlike poles of equal strength and separated by a small distance is called a magnetic dipole. In SI, the unit of magnetic pole strength is ampere metre (A.m).

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Dipole Moment: The product of the pole strength of either magnetic pole and the magnetic length of the magnetic dipole is called its magnetic dipole moment. The SI unit of magnetic dipole moment is ampere-metre 2 (A m 2 ).
text_html: Magnetic Dipole Moment: The product of the pole strength of either magnetic pole and the magnetic length of the magnetic dipole is called its magnetic dipole moment. The SI unit of magnetic dipole moment is ampere-metre2 (A m2).

subject: Physics
text: Current loop and a magnetic dipole: A current loop of area A carrying current I behave as a magnetic dipole having magnetic dipole moment: m = I {tex}\cdot{/tex} A
text_html: Current loop and a magnetic dipole: A current loop of area A carrying current I behave as a magnetic dipole having magnetic dipole moment: m = I{tex}\cdot{/tex}A

subject: Physics
text: Torque on a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field: When a magnetic dipole of magnetic dipole moment m is placed in a uniform magnetic field of strength B making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the magnetic field, it experiences a torque, which is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = m {tex}\times{/tex} B = mBsin {tex}\theta{/tex}
text_html:
Torque on a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field: When a magnetic dipole of magnetic dipole moment m is placed in a uniform magnetic field of strength B making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex}
with the direction of the magnetic field, it experiences a torque, which is given by {tex}\tau{/tex} = m {tex}\times{/tex} B = mBsin{tex}\theta{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Potential energy stored in a magnetic dipole on rotating inside a magnetic field: The work done in rotating a magnetic dipole against the torque acting on it when placed in a magnetic field is stored inside the magnetic dipole in the form of its potential energy. When the magnetic dipole is rotated from its initial position {tex}\theta_1{/tex} , to the final position {tex}\theta_2{/tex} , then the potential energy stored is given by U = mB(cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} )
text_html: Potential energy stored in a magnetic dipole on rotating inside a magnetic field: The work done in rotating a magnetic dipole against the torque acting on it when placed in a magnetic field is stored inside the magnetic dipole in the form of its potential energy. When the magnetic dipole is rotated from its initial position {tex}\theta_1{/tex}, to the final position {tex}\theta_2{/tex}, then the potential energy stored is given by U = mB(cos{tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos{tex}\theta_1{/tex})

subject: Physics
text: Bar Magnet's Analogy with Solenoid The pattern of iron filings around a bar magnet mirrors the magnetic field lines, indicating the magnet functions similar to a magnetic dipole. Like a solenoid, a bar magnet can be thought of as numerous circulating currents.
text_html:
Bar Magnet's Analogy with Solenoid
The pattern of iron filings around a bar magnet mirrors the magnetic field lines, indicating the magnet functions similar to a magnetic dipole. Like a solenoid, a bar magnet can be thought of as numerous circulating currents.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Poles Explained A bar magnet possesses two poles: the North and the South, analogous to the charges of an electric dipole. When freely suspended, these poles align towards the Earth's respective hemispheres.
text_html:
Magnetic Poles Explained
A bar magnet possesses two poles: the North and the South, analogous to the charges of an electric dipole. When freely suspended, these poles align towards the Earth's respective hemispheres.

subject: Physics
text: Properties of Magnetic Field Lines Magnetic field lines form continuous closed loops, unlike electric field lines. The tangent to a line shows the magnetic field's direction. The more lines per unit area, the stronger the magnetic field. Field lines don't intersect, ensuring a unique magnetic field direction.
text_html:
Properties of Magnetic Field Lines
  • Magnetic field lines form continuous closed loops, unlike electric field lines.
  • The tangent to a line shows the magnetic field's direction.
  • The more lines per unit area, the stronger the magnetic field.
  • Field lines don't intersect, ensuring a unique magnetic field direction.

subject: Physics
text: The Method to Plot Magnetic Field Lines One can plot magnetic field lines by moving a small magnetic compass around the bar magnet, checking impressions of the magnetic field direction at various points.
text_html:
The Method to Plot Magnetic Field Lines
One can plot magnetic field lines by moving a small magnetic compass around the bar magnet, checking impressions of the magnetic field direction at various points.

subject: Physics
text: Solenoid and Bar Magnet: Equivalent Fields Analyzing field similarities, we see that cutting a bar magnet mirrors cutting a solenoid into smaller units, maintaining field line continuity.
text_html:
Solenoid and Bar Magnet: Equivalent Fields
Analyzing field similarities, we see that cutting a bar magnet mirrors cutting a solenoid into smaller units, maintaining field line continuity.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Moment and Field Relationships The magnetic field at a point due to a solenoid can be expressed as: B= {tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 m}{r^3}{/tex} . This mirrors the far axial field of a bar magnet.
text_html:
Magnetic Moment and Field Relationships
The magnetic field at a point due to a solenoid can be expressed as:
B={tex}\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 m}{r^3}{/tex}. This mirrors the far axial field of a bar magnet.

subject: Physics
text: Oscillation in a Uniform Magnetic Field A compass needle oscillates due to torque when placed in a uniform magnetic field, expressed as: {tex}\tau={m} \times {B}{/tex} .
text_html:
Oscillation in a Uniform Magnetic Field
A compass needle oscillates due to torque when placed in a uniform magnetic field, expressed as:
{tex}\tau={m} \times {B}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Potential Energy Concept The magnetic potential energy of a dipole in a magnetic field is: {tex}U_m = -{m} \cdot {B}{/tex} , with energy relationships analogous to electrostatics.
text_html:
Magnetic Potential Energy Concept
The magnetic potential energy of a dipole in a magnetic field is:
{tex}U_m = -{m} \cdot {B}{/tex}, with energy relationships analogous to electrostatics.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic vs. Electric Dipoles Magnetic fields at large distances relate to formulas for electric dipoles, replacing terms accordingly. Equations represent similar force laws governing magnetic moments.
text_html:
Magnetic vs. Electric Dipoles
Magnetic fields at large distances relate to formulas for electric dipoles, replacing terms accordingly. Equations represent similar force laws governing magnetic moments.

subject: Physics
text: Comparison of Equatorial and Axial Fields The equatorial field of a far bar magnet is: {tex}B_E=-\frac{\mu_0 {m}}{4 \pi r^3}{/tex} , while its axial field is: {tex}B_A=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {m}}{r^3}{/tex} .
text_html:
Comparison of Equatorial and Axial Fields
The equatorial field of a far bar magnet is:
{tex}B_E=-\frac{\mu_0 {m}}{4 \pi r^3}{/tex}, while its axial field is:
{tex}B_A=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 {m}}{r^3}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss's Law for Electrostatics Chapter 1 introduces Gauss's law, focusing on electrostatics. In a diagram, surface (i) shows the number of lines leaving is equal to those entering. No net charge is enclosed within surface (i). For surface (ii), a net outward flux indicates the inclusion of net positive charge.
text_html:
Gauss's Law for Electrostatics
  • Chapter 1 introduces Gauss's law, focusing on electrostatics.
  • In a diagram, surface (i) shows the number of lines leaving is equal to those entering.
  • No net charge is enclosed within surface (i).
  • For surface (ii), a net outward flux indicates the inclusion of net positive charge.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Fields and Flux Magnetic fields differ from electric fields as they form closed loops. In diagrams, surfaces (i) and (ii) show balanced magnetic lines entering and leaving. Net magnetic flux is zero for any closed surface.
text_html:
Magnetic Fields and Flux
  • Magnetic fields differ from electric fields as they form closed loops.
  • In diagrams, surfaces (i) and (ii) show balanced magnetic lines entering and leaving.
  • Net magnetic flux is zero for any closed surface.

subject: Physics
text: Field Lines and Closed Surfaces Magnetic fields are illustrated by examining Gaussian surfaces. In each case, the number of entering and leaving lines is equal, ensuring no net flux. This characteristic applies to any closed surface.
text_html:
Field Lines and Closed Surfaces
  • Magnetic fields are illustrated by examining Gaussian surfaces.
  • In each case, the number of entering and leaving lines is equal, ensuring no net flux.
  • This characteristic applies to any closed surface.

subject: Physics
text: Small Vector Area Element - {tex} \Delta S {/tex} Consider a small vector area element, {tex} \Delta S {/tex} , on a closed surface, denoted as {tex} S {/tex} . Magnetic flux through {tex} \overrightarrow{{A}} {S} {/tex} is defined as {tex} \Delta \phi_B={B} \cdot \Delta {S} {/tex} .
text_html:
Small Vector Area Element - {tex} \Delta S {/tex}

  • Consider a small vector area element, {tex} \Delta S {/tex}, on a closed surface, denoted as {tex} S {/tex}.
  • Magnetic flux through {tex} \overrightarrow{{A}} {S} {/tex} is defined as {tex} \Delta \phi_B={B} \cdot \Delta {S} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Net Magnetic Flux Calculation Divide the surface into smaller elements to calculate individual flux for each. Net flux {tex} \phi_B {/tex} is given as: {tex} \phi_B=\sum {B} \cdot \Delta {S}=0 {/tex} .
text_html:
Net Magnetic Flux Calculation

  • Divide the surface into smaller elements to calculate individual flux for each.
  • Net flux {tex} \phi_B {/tex} is given as: {tex} \phi_B=\sum {B} \cdot \Delta {S}=0 {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Comparison with Electrostatics Gauss's law for electrostatics compares flux to charge enclosed. Flux expression: {tex} \sum {E} \cdot \Delta {S}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0} {/tex} , where {tex} q {/tex} is the enclosed charge.
text_html:
Comparison with Electrostatics

  • Gauss's law for electrostatics compares flux to charge enclosed.
  • Flux expression: {tex} \sum {E} \cdot \Delta {S}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0} {/tex}, where {tex} q {/tex} is the enclosed charge.

subject: Physics
text: Absence of Magnetic Monopoles Unlike electric charges, isolated magnetic poles do not exist. No sources or sinks for magnetic field {tex} {B} {/tex} .
text_html:
Absence of Magnetic Monopoles

  • Unlike electric charges, isolated magnetic poles do not exist.
  • No sources or sinks for magnetic field {tex} {B} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Dipoles and Current Loops All magnetic phenomena are explained using dipoles or current loops. Magnetic elements create closed loop patterns.
text_html:
Magnetic Dipoles and Current Loops
  • All magnetic phenomena are explained using dipoles or current loops.
  • Magnetic elements create closed loop patterns.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss's Law for Magnetism The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is always zero. This is a fundamental result of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.
text_html:
Gauss's Law for Magnetism
  • The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is always zero.
  • This is a fundamental result of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.

subject: Physics
text: Implications for Magnetic Fields Magnetic fields inherently exhibit closed-loop characteristics. This results in balanced entry and exit of the field lines for any closed system.
text_html:
Implications for Magnetic Fields

  • Magnetic fields inherently exhibit closed-loop characteristics.
  • This results in balanced entry and exit of the field lines for any closed system.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic intensity: It is defined as the ratio of magnetic induction in a vacuum to the absolute magnetic permeability of free space. It is given by H = {tex}\frac {\beta_0}{\mu_0}{/tex} Where tesla metre/ampere is the absolute permeability of vacuum. Magnetic intensity is also known as H-field or magnetising field strength. The unit of magnetic intensity i.e. {tex}\frac {A}{m}{/tex} is also equivalent to {tex}\frac {N}{m^2T}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {N}{Wb}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {J}{m^{-3}T}{/tex}
text_html:
Magnetic intensity: It is defined as the ratio of magnetic induction in a vacuum to the absolute magnetic permeability of free space. It is given by H = {tex}\frac {\beta_0}{\mu_0}{/tex}
Where tesla metre/ampere is the absolute permeability of vacuum. Magnetic intensity is also known as H-field or magnetising field strength. The unit of magnetic intensity i.e. {tex}\frac {A}{m}{/tex} is also equivalent to {tex}\frac {N}{m^2T}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {N}{Wb}{/tex} or {tex}\frac {J}{m^{-3}T}{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Intensity of magnetisation: It is defined as the magnetic dipole moment developed per unit volume M = {tex}\frac{m_{\text {net }}}{V}{/tex} In SI, the unit of intensity of magnetisation is ampere/metre (A/m).
text_html:
Intensity of magnetisation: It is defined as the magnetic dipole moment developed per unit volume
M = {tex}\frac{m_{\text {net }}}{V}{/tex}
In SI, the unit of intensity of magnetisation is ampere/metre (A/m).

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic induction: It is defined as the number of magnetic lines of induction (magnetic field lines inside the material) crossing per unit area normally through the magnetic material. If H is the strength of the magnetising field, then magnetic induction is given by B= {tex}\mu_0{/tex} (H + M) In SI, the unit of the strength of magnetising field is ampere/metre (A/m) and that of magnetic induction is tesla (T) or weber/metre 2 ( {tex}\frac {Wb}{m^2}{/tex} )
text_html:
Magnetic induction: It is defined as the number of magnetic lines of induction (magnetic field lines inside the material) crossing per unit area normally through the magnetic material. If H is the strength of the magnetising field, then magnetic induction is given by B= {tex}\mu_0{/tex}(H + M)
In SI, the unit of the strength of magnetising field is ampere/metre (A/m) and that of magnetic induction is tesla (T) or weber/metre2 ({tex}\frac {Wb}{m^2}{/tex})

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic susceptibility: The magnetic susceptibility of a material is defined as the ratio of the intensity of magnetisation (M) and the strength of magnetising field (H). It is given by {tex}\chi_m=\frac{M}{H}{/tex} It has no units.
text_html:
Magnetic susceptibility: The magnetic susceptibility of a material is defined as the ratio of the intensity of magnetisation (M) and the strength of magnetising field (H). It is given by {tex}\chi_m=\frac{M}{H}{/tex}
It has no units.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic permeability: The magnetic permeability of a material is defined as the ratio of the magnetic induction (B) of the material to the strength of magnetising field (H). It is given by {tex}\mu_m=\frac{B}{H}{/tex} In SI, the unit of magnetic permeability is tesla metre/ ampere ( {tex}T\frac {m}{A}{/tex} )
text_html:
Magnetic permeability: The magnetic permeability of a material is defined as the ratio of the magnetic induction (B) of the material to the strength of magnetising field (H). It is given by {tex}\mu_m=\frac{B}{H}{/tex}
In SI, the unit of magnetic permeability is tesla metre/ ampere ({tex}T\frac {m}{A}{/tex})

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Properties of Materials The earth is filled with a variety of elements and compounds, and humans have synthesized various new alloys, compounds, and even elements. All these substances have diverse magnetic properties.
text_html:
Magnetic Properties of Materials
The earth is filled with a variety of elements and compounds, and humans have synthesized various new alloys, compounds, and even elements. All these substances have diverse magnetic properties.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetisation (M) In a material, when the magnetic moments from circulating electrons are summed, they produce a net magnetic moment. Magnetisation is defined as this net magnetic moment divided by the volume: M = m net /V It is a vector quantity measured in A m -1 .
text_html:
Magnetisation (M)
In a material, when the magnetic moments from circulating electrons are summed, they produce a net magnetic moment. Magnetisation is defined as this net magnetic moment divided by the volume:
M = mnet/V
It is a vector quantity measured in A m-1.

subject: Physics
text: Internal Magnetic Field of a Solenoid A solenoid with n turns per unit length and current I generates a magnetic field inside represented as: B 0 = μ 0 nI
text_html:
Internal Magnetic Field of a Solenoid
A solenoid with n turns per unit length and current I generates a magnetic field inside represented as:
B0 = μ0nI

subject: Physics
text: Material Influence on Solenoid Magnetic Field When a solenoid is filled with a material with magnetisation: B = B 0 + B m B m = μ 0 M
text_html:
Material Influence on Solenoid Magnetic Field
When a solenoid is filled with a material with magnetisation:

  • B = B0 + Bm
  • Bm = μ0M

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Intensity (H) An additional vector field H, defined by: H = B/μ 0 - M It is measured in the same units as magnetisation.
text_html:
Magnetic Intensity (H)
An additional vector field H, defined by:
H = B/μ0 - M
It is measured in the same units as magnetisation.

subject: Physics
text: Total Magnetic Field Expression The total magnetic field, considering both external and material contributions, is: B = μ 0 (H + M)
text_html:
Total Magnetic Field Expression
The total magnetic field, considering both external and material contributions, is:
B = μ0(H + M)

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Susceptibility (χ) Susceptibility indicates how a material responds to an external magnetic field. It is a dimensionless quantity. Paramagnetic: χ > 0 Diamagnetic: χ
text_html:
Magnetic Susceptibility (χ)
Susceptibility indicates how a material responds to an external magnetic field. It is a dimensionless quantity.
  • Paramagnetic: χ > 0
  • Diamagnetic: χ < 0, and M is opposite to H

subject: Physics
text: Relationship of B, H, and χ From equations: M = χH B = μ 0 (1 + χ)H = μ 0 μ r H = μH
text_html:
Relationship of B, H, and χ
From equations:

  • M = χH
  • B = μ0(1 + χ)H = μ0μrH = μH

subject: Physics
text: Relative Magnetic Permeability (μ r ) Defined as 1 + χ, providing an analogy to dielectric constant in electrostatics. It interrelates with susceptibility and magnetic permeability.
text_html:
Relative Magnetic Permeability (μr)
Defined as 1 + χ, providing an analogy to dielectric constant in electrostatics.
It interrelates with susceptibility and magnetic permeability.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Permeability (μ) Having the same dimensions/units as μ 0 , it is described by: μ = μ 0 μ r = μ 0 (1 + χ)
text_html:
Magnetic Permeability (μ)
Having the same dimensions/units as μ0, it is described by:
μ = μ0μr = μ0(1 + χ)

subject: Physics
text: Diamagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are feebly magnetised in a direction opposite to that of the magnetising field.
text_html: Diamagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are feebly magnetised in a direction opposite to that of the magnetising field.

subject: Physics
text: Paramagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are feebly magnetised in the direction of the magnetising field.
text_html: Paramagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are feebly magnetised in the direction of the magnetising field.

subject: Physics
text: Ferromagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are strongly magnetised in the direction of the magnetising field.
text_html: Ferromagnetic substances: Those substances, which when placed in a magnetic field are strongly magnetised in the direction of the magnetising field.

subject: Physics
text: Classification of Magnetic Materials Materials can be classified into three types based on their magnetic properties: diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic. Diamagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is negative. Paramagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is positive but small. Ferromagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is large and positive.
text_html:
Classification of Magnetic Materials
Materials can be classified into three types based on their magnetic properties: diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic.

  • Diamagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is negative.
  • Paramagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is positive but small.
  • Ferromagnetic: Susceptibility {tex} \chi {/tex} is large and positive.

subject: Physics
text: Diamagnetism Characteristics Diamagnetic materials move from stronger to weaker parts of an external magnetic field. They are repelled by magnets. The induced magnetic moment is opposite to the applied field, causing slight repulsion. Common diamagnetic materials: Bismuth, copper, lead, water. Superconductors are perfect diamagnets with $$ \chi = -1 $$.
text_html:
Diamagnetism Characteristics
Diamagnetic materials move from stronger to weaker parts of an external magnetic field. They are repelled by magnets.

  • The induced magnetic moment is opposite to the applied field, causing slight repulsion.
  • Common diamagnetic materials: Bismuth, copper, lead, water.
  • Superconductors are perfect diamagnets with $$ \chi = -1 $$.

subject: Physics
text: Orbital Magnetic Moments in Diamagnetism Electrons in diamagnetic materials have zero resultant magnetic moment. When a magnetic field is applied, electrons adjust their speed to align against the field. This adjustment repels the external magnetic field according to Lenz's law.
text_html:
Orbital Magnetic Moments in Diamagnetism
Electrons in diamagnetic materials have zero resultant magnetic moment.

  • When a magnetic field is applied, electrons adjust their speed to align against the field.
  • This adjustment repels the external magnetic field according to Lenz's law.

subject: Physics
text: Paramagnetism Characteristics Paramagnetic materials are weakly attracted to magnets and move from weak to strong magnetic fields. Atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles. Higher magnetisation is achieved with \emph{low} temperatures or \emph{high} external fields. Common paramagnetic materials: Aluminium, sodium, oxygen.
text_html:
Paramagnetism Characteristics
Paramagnetic materials are weakly attracted to magnets and move from weak to strong magnetic fields.

  • Atoms have permanent magnetic dipoles.
  • Higher magnetisation is achieved with \emph{low} temperatures or \emph{high} external fields.
  • Common paramagnetic materials: Aluminium, sodium, oxygen.

subject: Physics
text: Temperature Impact on Paramagnetism Magnetisation in paramagnetic materials depends on temperature. As temperature lowers, magnetisation increases. Increased external field strengthens alignment of dipoles.
text_html:
Temperature Impact on Paramagnetism
Magnetisation in paramagnetic materials depends on temperature.

  • As temperature lowers, magnetisation increases.
  • Increased external field strengthens alignment of dipoles.

subject: Physics
text: Ferromagnetism Characteristics Ferromagnetic materials are strongly attracted to magnets and align along magnetic fields. Exhibit domain formation, where molecules self-align over macroscopic volume. Domains vary randomly without external magnetic influence. Common ferromagnetic elements: Iron, nickel, cobalt.
text_html:
Ferromagnetism Characteristics
Ferromagnetic materials are strongly attracted to magnets and align along magnetic fields.

  • Exhibit domain formation, where molecules self-align over macroscopic volume.
  • Domains vary randomly without external magnetic influence.
  • Common ferromagnetic elements: Iron, nickel, cobalt.

subject: Physics
text: Domain Concept in Ferromagnetism Domains are macroscopic volumes in ferromagnetic materials where molecules align. Initially, domains are randomly oriented. Applying external field aligns and enlarges domains.
text_html:
Domain Concept in Ferromagnetism
Domains are macroscopic volumes in ferromagnetic materials where molecules align.

  • Initially, domains are randomly oriented.
  • Applying external field aligns and enlarges domains.

subject: Physics
text: Hard vs. Soft Ferromagnets Ferromagnets can be classified into hard and soft based on persistence of magnetisation after external field removal. Hard ferromagnets: Retain magnetisation (e.g., Alnico, lodestone). Soft ferromagnets: Lose magnetisation (e.g., soft iron).
text_html:
Hard vs. Soft Ferromagnets
Ferromagnets can be classified into hard and soft based on persistence of magnetisation after external field removal.

  • Hard ferromagnets: Retain magnetisation (e.g., Alnico, lodestone).
  • Soft ferromagnets: Lose magnetisation (e.g., soft iron).

subject: Physics
text: Temperature and Ferromagnetism Increasing temperature can turn ferromagnetic materials into paramagnetic. High temperatures disintegrate domain structures. Magnetisation gradually disappears with rising temperature.
text_html:
Temperature and Ferromagnetism
Increasing temperature can turn ferromagnetic materials into paramagnetic.

  • High temperatures disintegrate domain structures.
  • Magnetisation gradually disappears with rising temperature.

subject: Physics
text: Meissner Effect in Diamagnetism Superconductors exhibit the Meissner effect, which is perfect diamagnetism. Field lines are completely expelled from superconductors. Superconductors repel magnets entirely.
text_html:
Meissner Effect in Diamagnetism
Superconductors exhibit the Meissner effect, which is perfect diamagnetism.

  • Field lines are completely expelled from superconductors.
  • Superconductors repel magnets entirely.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetism in Nature Magnetic phenomena are universal, affecting galaxies, atoms, humans, and animals due to magnetic fields from various sources.
text_html:
Magnetism in Nature
Magnetic phenomena are universal, affecting galaxies, atoms, humans, and animals due to magnetic fields from various sources.

subject: Physics
text: Origin of the Term 'Magnet' The term 'magnet' originates from Magnesia, a region in Greece known for its magnetic ore deposits as early as 600 BC.
text_html:
Origin of the Term 'Magnet'
The term 'magnet' originates from Magnesia, a region in Greece known for its magnetic ore deposits as early as 600 BC.

subject: Physics
text: Discovery of Magnetism from Electric Currents Moving charges or electric currents produce magnetic fields, a discovery credited to scientists like Oersted, Ampere, Biot, and Savart.
text_html:
Discovery of Magnetism from Electric Currents
Moving charges or electric currents produce magnetic fields, a discovery credited to scientists like Oersted, Ampere, Biot, and Savart.

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Nature of Earth Earth acts as a magnet with its magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.
text_html:
Magnetic Nature of Earth
Earth acts as a magnet with its magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.

subject: Physics
text: Bar Magnet Orientation When freely suspended, a bar magnet aligns in the north-south direction with its north-seeking end termed as the "north pole."
text_html:
Bar Magnet Orientation
When freely suspended, a bar magnet aligns in the north-south direction with its north-seeking end termed as the "north pole."

subject: Physics
text: Magnetic Forces Repulsion between like poles (north-north/south-south) of magnets. Attraction between opposite poles (north-south) of magnets.
text_html:
Magnetic Forces

  1. Repulsion between like poles (north-north/south-south) of magnets.
  2. Attraction between opposite poles (north-south) of magnets.

subject: Physics
text: Impossibility of Magnetic Monopoles Magnetic monopoles do not exist. Cutting a bar magnet results in two smaller magnets each with a north and south pole.
text_html:
Impossibility of Magnetic Monopoles
Magnetic monopoles do not exist. Cutting a bar magnet results in two smaller magnets each with a north and south pole.

subject: Physics
text: Magnet Creation It's feasible to create magnets from iron and its alloys due to their property of magnetization.
text_html:
Magnet Creation
It's feasible to create magnets from iron and its alloys due to their property of magnetization.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss’s Law of Magnetism Gauss's law states that the net magnetic flux through a closed surface is zero, reflecting the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.
text_html:
Gauss’s Law of Magnetism
Gauss's law states that the net magnetic flux through a closed surface is zero, reflecting the non-existence of magnetic monopoles.

subject: Physics
text: Types of Magnetism in Materials Paramagnetism: Temporary magnetization in materials due to external magnetic fields. Diamagnetism: Weak repulsion from external magnetic fields. Ferromagnetism: Strong magnetization of materials, retaining properties even after removing external fields.
text_html:
Types of Magnetism in Materials

  • Paramagnetism: Temporary magnetization in materials due to external magnetic fields.
  • Diamagnetism: Weak repulsion from external magnetic fields.
  • Ferromagnetism: Strong magnetization of materials, retaining properties even after removing external fields.
